g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Deques, a powerful generalization of queues and stacks.
A deque allows insertion and deletion at both front and rear ends, unlike standard queues.
g.text: insertion and deletion at BOTH ends - front and rear.
It generalizes both Stack (LIFO) and Queue (FIFO).

g.data-narration: Deques are essential for sliding window algorithms, palindrome checking, and implementing undo systems.
g.text: A single deque can simulate both
a stack AND a queue using different

g.data-narration: There are three main types of deques: general, input-restricted, and output-restricted.
This table shows which operations are allowed in each type - memorize for exams!
The four fundamental deque operations: insertFront, insertRear, deleteFront, and deleteRear.
Circular arrays wrap indices around using modulo, reusing freed spaces efficiently.
g.text: Why Circular? After insertFront/deleteFront, front index moves forward and
would run out of space. Modulo operator wraps indices around!

g.data-narration: The deque structure uses an array, front/rear indices, and a size counter for clean implementation.
insertFront moves front backward FIRST, then stores the element - a common exam trick!
insertRear stores at current rear position, THEN moves rear forward to the next free slot.
g.text: insertRear: Store First, Then Move Forward
Key: rear always points to next free slot, not last element!

g.data-narration: deleteFront retrieves the element at front, then moves front forward to the next element.
g.text: deleteFront: Get Element, Then Move Forward

g.data-narration: deleteRear moves rear backward FIRST to find the last element, then retrieves and removes it.
g.text: deleteRear: Move Backward First, Then Get!

g.data-narration: This trace shows how elements and indices change with each operation - essential for exams.
A deque can simulate a stack using insertFront+deleteFront or insertRear+deleteRear operations.
g.text: This is exactly how a regular queue works!

g.data-narration: Deques power palindrome checking, sliding window algorithms, and undo-redo systems in editors.
Remember: all deque operations are O(1), rear points to next free slot, and insertFront moves backward first!
g.text: • Rear pointer always points to next free slot
